commonIssues: Heat-intolerant: plants get leggy and decline once temperatures stay above roughly 24C (75F); commonly removed and replaced when summer heat arrives.
Susceptible to fungal leaf spots, black root/stem rot, anthracnose, powdery mildew, scab, rust and smut, especially in wet or poorly drained conditions.
Avoid watering in late afternoon or evening and avoid overhead watering, as wet foliage encourages disease.
Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers, which increase rot susceptibility; a balanced-to-low-N feed such as 5-10-10 is preferred.
Pests include aphids, spider mites, slugs, snails, cutworms and various caterpillars; deer, rabbits and squirrels may browse foliage and flowers.

Container minimum reflects a small, portable pot (about 30 cm / 12 in diameter or less) so plants can be moved to cooler shade as the sun intensifies; pansies are small (15-25 cm tall) and do not need a large root volume.

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Viola x wittrockiana: full sun to part shade (part afternoon shade in St. Louis), medium/consistently moist well-drained humus-rich soil, USDA zones 6-10, height 0.50-0.75 ft, bloom April-June, short-lived perennial grown as cool-season annual, succumbs to summer heat, susceptible to fungal leaf diseases and slugs/snails, used in window boxes and containers.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Viola x wittrockiana: humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil; medium water needs; container and window-box suitable; small habit ~6-9 in tall.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Viola x wittrockiana: plants succumb to summer heat and should be removed when temperatures rise; grown as cool-season annual; susceptible to fungal leaf diseases, slugs and snails.

Clemson HGIC 'Pansies and Johnny-Jump-Ups' - rich well-draining soil with organic matter, pH 5.4-5.8; water ~1 inch per week (continue when less than 1 inch of rain falls weekly); never water late afternoon/evening; avoid overhead watering; apply 5-10-10 in late fall and early spring; avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers; spacing 6-8 in (small-flowered) to 10-12 in (large-flowered); 2 in organic mulch; diseases include black root rot and fungal leaf spots; pests aphids, spider mites, slugs.

Clemson HGIC 'Pansies and Johnny-Jump-Ups' - fertilizer: mix granular slow-release at planting or apply 5-10-10 in late fall and early spring; avoid high-nitrogen formulas (increase rot susceptibility).

Clemson HGIC 'Pansies, Violas, and Panolas' - plant in fall in full sun and organically rich well-drained soil; water-soluble liquid fertilizer every two to three weeks; pansies flower less in extreme cold; avoid moisture-retention potting mixes in wet winters to prevent root rot; deer, rabbits and squirrels browse plants.

UF/IFAS FPS-609 Viola x wittrockiana - part shade/part sun with shade during hot weather; needs ample moisture; tolerates clay, sand, acidic and loam soils, prefers rich soil; height 0.5-1 ft, spread 0.5-1 ft, round spreading form; thrives in winter in zones 8-11, not suitable for summer there; seed germinates 10-14 days at 65-75F; pests include aphids, cutworms, slugs, mites; diseases anthracnose, leaf spots, powdery mildew, scab, stem rot, rust, smut; used in containers, planters, edging, borders; spacing 6-12 in.

Old Farmer's Almanac 'Pansies' - hardy in USDA zones 3-8; full sun to part shade; keep soil consistently moist, water when the top inch of soil feels dry; heavy feeders, fertilize with water-soluble liquid fertilizer every two to three weeks; tolerate light frost and temperatures to about 25F (-4C); get leggy in summer heat (decline above ~75F/24C); grow best at soil temperatures 45-65F (7-18C); plant in early spring or fall; portable containers 12 in diameter or less recommended so plants can be moved to cooler areas.

Old Farmer's Almanac 'Pansies' - watering: keep soil consistently moist but not soggy, water when the top inch of soil feels dry, especially during dry spells; container soil dries faster than garden soil; light: full sun to part shade.

Old Farmer's Almanac 'Pansies' - portable containers 12 inches or less in diameter recommended (about 5 liters minimum) so plants can be moved to cooler shade; standard potting soil; light: full sun to part shade with morning sun preferred.

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays=3 is a conservative interpretation of 'water when the top inch is dry' / '~1 inch per week' (Almanac, Clemson); actual interval varies with weather/pot size. feedingProfile baseIntervalDays=18 is the midpoint of the cited 'every two to three weeks' (14-21 days) liquid-feed guidance (Almanac, Clemson). Light targetSunHours=5 is a mid-range estimate for the cited 'full sun to part shade / morning sun with afternoon shade in heat' (no exact hour count is published). containerProfile liters are estimated from the cited '12-inch-or-less diameter portable pot' since extensions give pot diameter, not volume. verticalSuitability=0.5 reflects documented suitability for window boxes and hanging containers without an authoritative numeric rating.
